results.explanation: The red shaded areas at the bottom of each figure do not proportionally match their labeled percentage values. For example, the 30% figure appears to have a similar or smaller red area compared to the 21% figure, which misrepresents the actual data differences. The visual sizes of the red sections do not accurately correspond to the percentages shown.

The y-axis starts at 19,400 instead of zero, visually exaggerating the differences between enrollment values across years. The actual variation in enrollment (approximately 1,000-1,200 thousand students) appears much more dramatic than it truly is due to the compressed scale.

A pie chart is used to show what appears to be three separate quantities (38,462, 26,923, and 34,615) that do not represent parts of a meaningful whole. These values do not sum to a logical total, and pie charts are only appropriate for part-to-whole relationships where components sum to 100% or a complete total.

The y-axis uses a logarithmic or heavily compressed scale (1, 3, 12, 42, 148, 516, 1803) rather than a linear scale starting at zero. This non-standard scaling distorts the visual comparison between the two metrics, making the differences appear more dramatic than they actually are.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales—the left axis ranges from 0-60 for assignment scores, while the right axis ranges from 10%-100% for heterozygosity. This dual-axis setup can mislead viewers by allowing the visual relationship between the lines to be manipulated based on axis scaling, making it difficult to accurately compare the two variables.

This is a histogram with a y-axis starting at 0 and evenly spaced tick intervals (5, 10, 15, 20, etc.), displaying a distribution across sequential bins on the x-axis. The chart appropriately represents the data without exaggeration or distortion.

The 3D pie chart distorts the visual representation of the data through perspective effects, making segments appear disproportionate to their labeled values. The 'YES 47%' segment appears visually larger than the 'NO 43%' segment despite only a 4-percentage-point difference, exaggerating the distinction between the two values.

This horizontal bar chart appropriately compares claims paid versus claims received across multiple years. The x-axis starts at 0, has consistent tick intervals (50-unit increments), and the bar lengths accurately represent their labeled values. The chart type is suitable for comparing two categorical variables across time periods.

The white bar in the $60,000 to $69,999 salary range is labeled '22' but visually extends to approximately 23-24 on the y-axis, misrepresenting its actual value. Additionally, several bars show inconsistencies between their labeled values and visual heights, such as the bars labeled '8' in the $60,000-$69,999 and $70,000-$79,999 ranges appearing to reach different visual heights despite identical labels.

The legend bins have inconsistent width ranges, varying from 0.14% to 1.29%, which could distort the visual perception of differences between regions. The unequal bin sizes make it difficult to fairly compare regional values and may exaggerate or minimize perceived differences in the underlying data.

The red stars placed on the map appear to mark specific locations with political significance rather than being based on the data values shown in the legend. The overlay text explicitly links criminality increases to the 'SP Forum' governance, suggesting the map's visual emphasis (red stars) is designed to support a political narrative rather than objectively represent the homicide rate variation data. This creates a false impression by using non-data visual elements to direct interpretation toward a predetermined conclusion.

The 3D perspective effect distorts the visual comparison of pie slices. The tilted, three-dimensional rendering makes the segments appear disproportionate to their labeled values, with the foreground slice (Television at 50%) appearing larger and the background slices appearing smaller than they should relative to each other.

The y-axis starts at 950 deals instead of 0, visually exaggerating the decline in venture rounds. This truncation makes the 22% drop from Q4 '14 to Q4 '15 appear much more dramatic than it actually is when viewed proportionally against the full range of possible values.

The x-axis starts at approximately 87.4 instead of 0, severely exaggerating the visual difference between the two performance metrics. This truncation makes the AMD FX overclocked result appear dramatically larger relative to the Intel Core i9 result than the actual data difference warrants.

The visual sizes of the bars do not accurately represent the labeled values. The Private sector bar (1,444 cases) appears disproportionately large compared to the Public sector bar (285 cases), with a visual ratio that significantly exaggerates the actual numerical difference of approximately 5:1. The rounded corners and bar proportions create a visual misrepresentation of the data.

The Star Wars bar is significantly longer than the Star Trek bar, but the ratio of visual length does not match the ratio of labeled values (53,636 ÷ 15,904 ≈ 3.4x, yet the bar appears roughly 3.5-4x longer visually). The bar lengths appear to exaggerate the difference between the two values through improper scaling or sizing.

This donut chart displays fraud detection methods that do not represent parts of a meaningful whole. The percentages (6.9%, 2.6%, 2.1%, 7.5%) appear to be independent metrics or rates rather than components that sum to 100%, making a pie/donut chart an inappropriate visualization choice. A bar chart would be more suitable for comparing these distinct categorical values.

The y-axis is inverted, with values increasing downward (0 at top, 35 at bottom), which reverses the perceived trend of the data. This inverted orientation creates a counterintuitive visualization where declining values appear to trend upward visually, potentially misleading viewers about the direction of change in confirmed cases, probable cases, and recovered cases over time.

The counties are ordered by date rather than by the magnitude of COVID-19 cases, making it difficult to visually identify which counties truly have the greatest number of confirmed cases as the title claims. The title promises a ranking of 'Top 5 Counties' but the x-axis organization by date obscures this comparison rather than highlighting it.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales (left axis for Bank assets up to 9,000 Euros bns, right axis for GDP up to 3,000 Euros bns), making direct visual comparison between the two measures misleading. The different scales distort the relationship between bank assets and GDP across countries.

The bar heights do not accurately match their labeled values. The first bar (Middle school) appears to reach approximately 70 on the y-axis but is labeled as 68, and the visual proportions between bars do not consistently correspond to their labeled percentages, creating a misrepresentation of the survey results.

The three circular segments do not accurately represent their labeled percentages. The yellow circle labeled '92%' appears disproportionately large compared to the other segments, and the visual sizes of the circles do not correspond to the stated values of 1%, 92%, and 7%. The circles should be sized proportionally to their percentages, but they are not.

The bar chart shows 3744 MW and 6753 MW for the two time periods, representing an 80.4% increase. However, the visual heights of the bars do not accurately match these values - the red bar appears disproportionately taller than an 80.4% increase should warrant, exaggerating the visual growth. The y-axis scaling and bar proportions do not align with the actual numerical difference between the values.

The bar chart misrepresents the data through two mechanisms: first, the visual height of the bars does not proportionally match their labeled values (35% vs 39.6%), with the right bar appearing disproportionately larger than a 4.6% difference should warrant; second, the y-axis appears to start above 0% rather than at zero, exaggerating the visual difference between the two values. This combination creates a false impression of a dramatic increase rather than a modest 4.6 percentage point rise.

A line chart is inappropriate for displaying years as a categorical variable rather than sequential time-series data with meaningful progression. Additionally, the y-axis starts at 0 but the data range (approximately 1900-2100 on x-axis with values around 2000 on y-axis) suggests the axis may be scaled to exaggerate or minimize perceived changes in what appears to be relatively flat data.

The 3D perspective distorts the visual comparison, making the orange slice appear larger than its 72% value relative to the blue 28% slice. Additionally, a pie chart is inappropriate for this data as the categories (Accurate vs. Misleading) represent a binary classification of coverage quality rather than parts of a meaningful whole, and the percentages are derived from segment counts rather than a natural composition.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales: the left axis measures count (0-50) while the right axis measures percentage (0%-100%). This dual-axis design makes it difficult to fairly compare the bar chart (# of Complaints) and line chart (Cumulative %) directly, potentially misleading viewers about their relative magnitudes and relationship.

The y-axis starts at 9100 instead of 0, which artificially exaggerates the differences between the bars. The actual differences between groups are much smaller than the visual representation suggests, making small variations appear dramatic.

The 3D perspective distorts the visual comparison of slice sizes, making some segments appear larger or smaller than their labeled values represent. Additionally, pie charts are inappropriate for survey results that do not represent parts of a meaningful whole—these percentages appear to be independent response distributions rather than components summing to 100%.

The map uses an appropriate color gradient to represent fraud rates across states, with consistent binning intervals (8%, 8-12%, 12-16%, 16-20%, >20%) clearly defined in the legend. The accompanying table provides exact fraud rates for verification. The visualization accurately represents the data without distortion or exaggeration.

This pie chart displays pizza topping preferences as percentages that sum to over 500%, not 100%. Pizza toppings are not parts of a whole—they represent independent preferences where multiple toppings can be selected simultaneously. A pie chart is fundamentally unsuitable for this type of data, which should instead be shown as a bar chart or other comparison format.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales (left axis: 83-87%, right axis: 55-95%), which distorts the visual comparison between the two students' scores. The different scale ranges make Adam's line appear to have steeper growth than John's, even though their actual performance trends are more similar than the visualization suggests.

A pie chart is used to show parts of a whole that sum to 100%, but these statistics sum to 245% (71% + 56% + 24% + 39% + 55%), indicating overlapping or independent metrics that represent different aspects of buyer persona statistics rather than a cohesive whole. Pie charts are inappropriate for displaying multiple independent percentages that do not represent parts of a single entity.

The y-axis begins at 0 but the visual area chart starts from a baseline well above zero, creating a disproportionate visual representation of the growth. The filled area below the curve exaggerates the magnitude of cases by making the rise appear steeper and more dramatic than the actual data values warrant. This is a common technique to visually amplify trends.

The bar chart appropriately displays literacy rate data with a y-axis starting at 0%, evenly spaced percentage intervals, and clearly labeled values on each bar. The chart type is suitable for comparing values across discrete time periods, and the axis scaling accurately represents the data without exaggeration.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales to compare identity fraud victims (left axis, 0-16 millions) and fraud losses (right axis, $0-$50 billions). This dual-axis design makes it difficult to fairly compare the two metrics and can create misleading visual impressions of their relative trends and magnitudes.

The y-axis begins at 196 mg/dL rather than zero, visually exaggerating the differences between weekly cholesterol measurements. This truncation makes the Week 1 value appear dramatically larger than Week 4, when the actual difference is relatively modest (approximately 9 mg/dL or 4.4%).

The 3D pie chart distorts visual comparison as slices closer to the viewer appear larger than they actually are. Additionally, while the data represents categorical counts of fraud cases, a pie chart is inappropriate here since these are independent categories of defendants filed, not parts of a meaningful whole that should sum to 100%—a bar chart would be more suitable for comparing category frequencies.

The bar lengths do not accurately represent the labeled values. For example, the 'One' bar labeled 600,000 appears only slightly longer than the 'Two' bar labeled 500,000, when it should be 20% longer. Similarly, 'Five' with a value of 170 has a visible bar when it should be nearly imperceptible compared to 600,000, indicating the bars are not proportionally scaled to their values.

The 3D circular design distorts visual comparison between the two salary values ($3328 vs $1404). The red circle for federal employee salaries is disproportionately larger than the visual ratio of the actual values (approximately 2.4:1), making the difference appear more dramatic than it is. The 3D effect further exaggerates the size perception of the larger value.

The y-axis starts at $249,000 instead of $0, dramatically exaggerating the visual difference between the 2014 and 2015 bars. The actual price increase appears to be approximately $2,000 (from ~$250,000 to ~$252,000), but the truncated axis makes it look like a massive spike. This violates the principle that bar chart axes should start at zero to accurately represent proportional differences.

The 3D map visualization with stacked, layered regions severely distorts visual comparison of the labeled percentages. The three-dimensional perspective causes regions in the foreground to appear larger than those in the background, making it impossible to accurately compare values by area. Additionally, the complex layering and 3D effects make it difficult to verify that the visual sizes correspond to the labeled percentages.

The 3D pie chart distorts visual comparison through perspective effects, making the foreground segment (TIMES NOW at 34%) appear disproportionately larger than its actual value. Additionally, the labeled percentages (20% + 30% + 34% + 16% = 100%) do not match the visual sizes of the segments due to the 3D rendering, which exaggerates certain slices.

The y-axis is inverted with negative values increasing downward, which reverses the natural perception of the trend. This makes a worsening deficit (increasingly negative cumulative values) appear to trend downward visually, potentially obscuring the severity of the growing deficit from casual viewers.

The y-axis starts at 0.5 instead of 0, which exaggerates the visual differences between the actual and expected SLUG% values. This truncation makes relatively small differences in performance metrics appear much more dramatic than they actually are.

The 'Chefs Plate' is prominently displayed at the top with a price of $5 per serving, while the other menu items (Goodfood, Chef CookIt, and MissFresh) are priced at $11.49-$11.50. The visual hierarchy and size of the Chefs Plate image disproportionately emphasizes this item despite it being significantly cheaper, creating a misleading impression about the primary offering and value proposition.

A line chart is used to display unemployment data across four selected quarters, which are categorical time periods rather than continuous sequential data. Line charts are designed to show trends over continuous variables, not discrete categorical periods with uneven time intervals (Dec '07, Sept '08, March '09, June '10). A bar chart would be more appropriate for comparing values across these specific quarters.

The bar chart showing 'DAMAGE IN LAST TEAM FIGHT' displays blue and red bars with labeled values, but the bar lengths do not accurately represent the numerical values shown. For example, the value 9879 has a shorter bar than 8538, contradicting the labeled data. The visual sizes of the bars do not proportionally match their corresponding damage values.

The y-axis starts at 48% instead of 0%, which artificially exaggerates the visual difference between the 60% value in 2010 and the 50% value in 2013. The 10 percentage point difference appears much larger than it actually is due to the compressed scale.

A pie chart is used to show percentages of workers across different countries who agree with a statement about manager knowledge. These percentages do not represent parts of a meaningful whole—they are independent country-level measurements that should be compared directly, making a bar chart more appropriate. Pie charts are designed for part-to-whole relationships, which does not apply here.

The chart uses stacked bars to show country distribution of 'Major Legends,' which is unsuitable for part-to-whole comparison and makes individual values difficult to compare across countries. Additionally, the visual segmentation and color patterns create misrepresentation where the relative sizes of segments do not clearly correspond to the underlying data values, particularly for countries with multiple colored segments.

The y-axis begins at 50% rather than 0%, artificially exaggerating the visual differences between political parties. The Democratic bar appears roughly 4 times taller than the Republican and Independent bars, when the actual difference is only about 10 percentage points (62% vs 53%).

The line chart appropriately displays obesity trends over time with a y-axis starting at 0, consistent and clearly labeled time intervals, and appropriate chart type for sequential data. The labeled values on the chart correspond to the visual positions of the lines, and the title accurately reflects the data showing obesity more than doubled for adults (14.5% to 34.3%) over 30 years.

The y-axis starts at 3000 instead of 0, which exaggerates the visual differences between the libraries. While Library A has approximately 3500 books and Library C has approximately 3150 books, the truncated axis makes Library C appear drastically smaller than it actually is relative to Library A.

A pie chart is used to display what appears to be survey response categories or data types that do not represent parts of a meaningful whole. The segments (Mobile Viewer score, Facebook Consideration, Density score, etc.) are distinct metrics or categories rather than components that sum to a complete entity, making a pie chart an inappropriate choice for this data.

The bar chart uses 3D effects that distort visual comparison between the two values. Additionally, the y-axis appears to be truncated or improperly scaled, as the visual height difference between the 7M and 6M bars is disproportionately large—the bars should only differ by about 14% in height, but they appear to differ by roughly 15-20% or more due to the 3D perspective and axis treatment.

The y-axis (Volume Displaced in cm³) does not start at zero, beginning instead around 29, which exaggerates the visual differences between data points. This truncation makes the linear relationship appear more dramatic than it actually is. For a scientific measurement like volume displacement, starting from zero is the appropriate baseline.

The bar lengths do not accurately represent their labeled values. For example, the bar for rating 'D' (55-68) appears visually similar in length to the bar for rating 'F' (21-38), despite D having a significantly higher numeric range. The visual bar sizes do not proportionally match the numerical values they represent.

The chart uses 3D perspective effects on the bars, which distorts visual comparison by making bars appear different sizes depending on their position in the 3D space. The depth and angle of the 3D rendering can exaggerate or minimize differences between the 2010 and 2011 values, particularly noticeable in the Handguns category where the bars appear artificially enlarged.

The bar lengths do not accurately represent the labeled dollar values. For example, the 2015-16 'Funds Leveraged' bar ($60,961,031) appears significantly longer than the 2013-14 bar ($40,458,337), but the visual difference is much more exaggerated than the ~50% actual difference in values. The bars are not proportionally scaled to their labeled amounts, creating a distorted visual comparison.

The 3D stacked bar chart distorts visual comparison of the spending categories through perspective effects, making it difficult to accurately compare segment sizes. The three-dimensional rendering causes the front faces of bars to appear larger than they actually are relative to the labeled values, misrepresenting the true proportions of each expenditure category.

The y-axis begins at 81% instead of 0%, which artificially exaggerates the visual difference between the graduation rates for 1991 (83%) and 1992 (87%). This compression makes a 4 percentage point increase appear dramatically larger than it actually is.

The y-axis is inverted, with negative values at the top and positive values at the bottom, which reverses the perceived trend and creates confusion. Additionally, the y-axis does not start at zero but rather at extreme negative values (-10000), exaggerating visual differences and distorting the comparison of the stacked area components.

The y-axis starts at $0 but the scale appears compressed at the lower end, and the visual spacing does not proportionally represent the values shown. The gap between the blue (Ending WAC) and orange (Average revenue) bars is visually exaggerated relative to their actual numerical differences, particularly in earlier years where the differences are small but appear visually large.

This line chart appropriately displays cumulative adoption trends over time (1960-2008) for five Australian regions. The y-axis starts at 0 and reaches 100%, with evenly spaced tick intervals, and the sequential temporal data is suitable for line chart representation. No misleading elements are detected.

The labeled percentages do not sum to 100% (39.0% + 19.5% + 9.8% + 7.4% + 3.1% + 21.2% = 100.0%, though the visual representation appears inconsistent with the labeled values). More critically, the 3D pie chart effect distorts the visual comparison, making segments appear disproportionate to their actual values, particularly exaggerating the apparent size of the purple 'Other' segment.

The 3D perspective effect distorts the visual comparison between the two pie slices. The dark blue slice (4.0%) appears exaggerated due to the 3D angle, while the turquoise slice (96.0%) is visually minimized, creating a misleading impression of their relative proportions.

This line chart appropriately displays the relationship between identification strength and Brexit attitudes across four categories. The y-axis starts at 0% and uses consistent intervals, the x-axis represents an ordinal scale (strength of identification from 1-7) in logical order, and the line chart is suitable for showing trends across categories. All elements are properly scaled and labeled.

The line chart displays two intersecting trend lines with labeled values, but the visual heights of the data points do not accurately represent the magnitudes of the numbers. For example, the red line starts at 289,750 and ends at 327,000 (a small increase), yet appears to rise significantly on the chart. Meanwhile, the purple line drops from 2,007,731 to 935,573 (a massive decrease of over 1 million), but visually appears to decline only slightly. The visual representation grossly mismatches the actual data proportions.

The 3D perspective effect distorts the visual comparison of bar sizes, making it difficult to accurately assess the true proportions. The labeled percentages (69.5%, 18.5%, 12.0%) do not appear to match the visual heights of the bars due to the 3D rendering, which exaggerates the front bars and minimizes the back bars.

The y-axis starts at 34 instead of 0, exaggerating the visual magnitude of the increase from 35% to 39.6%. This truncation makes the rise appear much more dramatic than it actually is—a 4.6 percentage point increase is displayed as if it were a massive change.

The chart uses two y-axes with dramatically different scales (left axis: 0-50 for P/E ratio, right axis: 0-18 for interest rates), which can distort visual comparisons between the two variables. The scales are not proportionally balanced, making it difficult to accurately assess the relationship between price-earnings ratios and long-term interest rates.

The bar lengths do not accurately represent the labeled values. The chicken bar (1.200.000.000) is visually much smaller than the dog bar (7.200.000), despite representing a value roughly 166 times larger. The visual scaling of the bars is severely distorted compared to their actual numeric values.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales (left axis: 0-20% for Aid/GNI, right axis: -1.0 to 2.5% for Growth GDP/Capita), which distorts visual comparison between the two variables. The dual axes can create false impressions about the relationship between aid and growth by allowing independent scaling that exaggerates or minimizes apparent correlations.

The chart uses multiple y-axes with different scales for different commodities (green for Economist Commodity Index, gray for Silver, orange for Copper, and black for Crude Oil), making direct visual comparisons between the series misleading. Each commodity has its own scale optimized to fill the chart space, distorting the true relative magnitudes and trends across commodities.

Line charts are inappropriate for comparing non-sequential categorical data (two separate procedures) across time periods. The crossing lines create a false visual impression of equivalence and trend intersection that misrepresents the actual independent trends of abortions versus preventive procedures. The line format suggests a causal or comparative relationship between the two unrelated data series that should not be implied.

The line chart displays sequential monthly data with a y-axis starting at $0.00, evenly spaced tick intervals, and appropriate chart type for time-series visualization. No misleading elements are detected.

The stacked bar chart shows 'Vaccinated' and 'People' as components, but the legend and visual representation are confusing. The orange 'Vaccinated' segments do not appear to represent proportions of the green 'People' bars, and the bars do not stack to consistent totals, making it unclear what the actual values and relationships are. The chart structure suggests a part-to-whole comparison that is not clearly validated by the data representation.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales—the left axis shows absolute abortion counts (400,000-520,000) while the right axis shows percentage change (-5.0% to 2.0%). This dual axis presentation makes it difficult to fairly compare the two metrics and can create misleading visual impressions of their relationship. The two lines appear to have similar magnitude changes when in fact they represent vastly different measures on incomparable scales.

The y-axis starts at 0 but extends to 1000, while the actual data values range only between approximately 0-100. This creates a massively exaggerated axis range that compresses the visual representation of the data, making meaningful variations between mean and median appear insignificant and harder to interpret.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales—the left axis for white (blue line) ranges from 50-58, while the right axis for black (red line) ranges from 100-160 deaths per 100,000. This dual-axis approach with disparate scales distorts visual comparison and exaggerates the differences between the two groups' mortality trends.

The y-axis starts at 8% instead of 0%, exaggerating the visual differences in unemployment rate fluctuations throughout 2011. This truncation makes relatively small variations (ranging from 8.6% to 9.2%) appear much more dramatic than they actually are.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales—the left axis for Trials (ranging from 900 to 1.8K) and the right axis for Subscriptions (ranging from 45 to 60 on the left chart, and 30 to 90 on the right chart). This dual-axis approach with disparate scales can distort visual comparisons between the two metrics, making it difficult to accurately perceive their relative magnitudes and trends.

The y-axis starts at 40,000 rather than zero, visually exaggerating the magnitude of fluctuations in first-year enrollment over time. This truncation makes the sharp decline at the end appear more dramatic than it actually is in relative terms.

The y-axis starts at 10° instead of 0°, exaggerating the visual appearance of temperature changes over time. Additionally, the title explicitly states '50x scale,' which artificially amplifies the perceived magnitude of variations in global temperature, making modest changes appear dramatic.

The bar chart displays food waste data (liquids in quarts) with a y-axis starting at 0 and evenly spaced tick intervals. The labeled values (14 qt, 17.5 qt, 12 qt) accurately match the bar heights, and the per-guest calculations are provided for context. The chart uses an appropriate format for comparing quantities across different time periods.

The color gradient legend at the bottom uses a scale from approximately 47.6% to 52.6%, which compresses a narrow 5-percentage-point range across the entire color spectrum. This exaggerates visual differences between states with similar female population percentages (e.g., states at 50.3% and 51.1% appear dramatically different in color despite only a 0.8% difference). The top map's labeling of 51.1% and 51.6% as representing distinctly 'male' and 'female' states further misrepresents what are actually very minor demographic differences.

The stacked area chart uses an inappropriate visualization for this data. The massive green area (representing ~99-99.5% of the population surviving) dominates the visual space, making the actual metric of interest—deaths as a percentage—nearly invisible and impossible to interpret accurately. The visual representation drastically misrepresents the relative importance of the data being shown, as the tiny red sliver for deaths is completely overwhelmed by the survival area.

The choropleth map uses color intensity to represent population growth ranges, but the visual perception of color saturation does not accurately correspond to the binned values. Michigan is colored in bright red to indicate <0% growth, yet appears visually more prominent than states with much higher growth rates (15-20%+), creating a misrepresentation of relative magnitudes through inappropriate color choice and saturation.

The income brackets shown on the x-axis have inconsistent widths, ranging from $10,000 intervals (Under $15,000 to $24,999) to $25,000 intervals ($25,000 to $49,999) to $50,000 intervals ($50,000 to $99,999) and even $100,000+ at the end. Without normalization, this unequal binning distorts the visual comparison of percent distribution across income levels, making some brackets appear more significant than they actually are.

The chart uses two metrics (Deaths per 100K Residents and Stringency Score) normalized to a 0-1 scale, making direct visual comparison misleading. The normalization obscures the actual relationship between gun deaths and gun law strictness by rescaling both variables to fit the same axis, preventing viewers from understanding the true magnitude and correlation of the underlying data.

A line chart is inappropriate for displaying categorical data (different restaurant activities) that has no sequential or temporal relationship. Additionally, the y-axis starts at 0% but is truncated at the lower end, with the scale jumping to 20%, exaggerating the visual differences between the relatively close data points.

The chart uses a 3D pie chart to display ski trail difficulty percentages, which distorts visual comparison due to the 3D perspective effect. Additionally, while these percentages do represent parts of a whole (100% of trails), the 3D rendering makes it difficult to accurately assess the relative sizes of each slice, with the front slices appearing disproportionately larger than they should be.

The y-axis starts at 0 but has inconsistent spacing between tick marks (0, 2500, 5000, 7500, 10000, 12500), which compresses the lower values and exaggerates the visual differences between the early cases (2-821) and later cases (3,916-12,462). The small initial values appear even more negligible compared to the dramatic heights of the final bars.

The bars are not ordered chronologically by date, with '29 sep' and '24h' appearing out of sequence at the end, which disrupts the natural temporal flow and could mislead viewers about trend progression. Additionally, the bar heights do not accurately represent their labeled values—for example, the bar for '29 sep' (784.41) appears taller than '18 sep' (682.57), but the visual difference is exaggerated beyond what the numerical difference warrants.

The chart uses 3D effects that distort visual comparison between bars, making it difficult to accurately assess relative heights. Additionally, the 3D perspective causes the bar sizes to not properly correspond to their labeled values, with the depth dimension creating visual misrepresentation of the data.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales: the left axis shows bhp (DIN) ranging from 10-50, while the right axis shows mkg (lb.ft.) with a different scale (6-11 with values in parentheses). This dual-axis setup can mislead viewers about the relative magnitudes and relationships between the two curves, as the scales are independently set and not proportionally comparable.

The 3D perspective effect distorts visual comparison of bar heights, making the 2012 bar (55.3°F) appear disproportionately larger than its actual value relative to others. The three-dimensional rendering causes the bars to not align properly with their labeled values on the y-axis, exaggerating the difference between 2012 and previous years.

The chart uses two y-axes with dramatically different scales ($0-$100,000 on the left for commissions vs. $0-$2,000,000 on the right for sales), which distorts the visual comparison between the two metrics. This scale disparity makes it difficult to accurately perceive the relationship between Plaintiff's Commissions and Defendant's Sales, as the same percentage change appears vastly different depending on which axis is used.

The pie chart displays three separate survey responses (55%, 31%, and 55%) that do not represent parts of a single whole, making a pie chart inappropriate for this data. Additionally, the visual sizes of the pie segments do not accurately correspond to their labeled percentages, with the segments appearing disproportionate to the values they represent.

The y-axis starts at 10 instead of 0, which exaggerates the visual increase in the percentage of Hartford minority students in reduced-isolation settings. This truncation makes the growth between 2002-03 and 2006-07 appear much more dramatic than it actually is (approximately 11% to 18% represents only a 7 percentage point increase).

This is a choropleth map appropriately using color encoding to represent categorical data (age groups with largest poverty rate increases) across geographic regions. The legend clearly identifies each age group category, the data source is cited, and the map format is suitable for displaying geographic variation. No axis manipulation, inappropriate chart type selection, or visual distortion is present.

The y-axis starts at 0 but extends to 5000 pounds, while the actual data values appear to range only from approximately 0 to 300 pounds. This massive unused axis range compresses the visual representation of the data, making what appears to be a relatively stable trend look artificially flat and minimizing the perception of any variations in lifetime weight gain across the time period shown.

The y-axis starts at approximately 100.0 rather than 0, dramatically exaggerating the visual differences between the Stoxx 600 and Tech sector lines. The sharp spike at the end appears much more dramatic than the actual ~0.8 point change, creating a false impression of volatility.

This stacked bar chart appropriately displays cumulative CO2 emissions data across countries and time periods. The y-axis starts at zero, ticks are evenly spaced, and the stacked bar format is suitable for showing part-to-whole composition across categories. The legend clearly identifies each time period, and the visual representation accurately matches the labeled values.

This bubble chart appropriately uses color gradients to represent percentage changes and y-axis position to show the count of sectors gaining or losing jobs. The y-axis is properly scaled from -20 to 20, the color legend clearly indicates the mapping from -1% to +0.75%, and the visualization method is suitable for tracking multiple dimensions (time, sector count, and percentage change) simultaneously.

A line chart is used to display productivity across categorical fertilizer types (A through G), which are not sequential or continuous data. This chart type implies a continuous progression or time-series relationship that doesn't exist, making it inappropriate for comparing discrete categories.

The color-coding scheme does not account for population size differences between countries, making visual comparisons misleading. A country with 10,000+ cases shown in dark red appears equally severe as another country with 10,000+ cases, despite vastly different population sizes and case rates. The map represents absolute case counts rather than per-capita rates, distorting the actual severity of outbreaks across regions.

A line chart is used to display data across categorical fruit types (Oranges, Apples, Pears, Bananas, Pineapples) rather than sequential or continuous data. Line charts are designed to show trends over time or continuous variables, not to compare values across unrelated categories, which would be better represented as a bar chart or grouped visualization.

This is a choropleth map showing COVID-19 cases by state with appropriate color-coding for different case ranges. The legend clearly defines each color category, the title accurately describes the data, and the visualization method is suitable for displaying geographic distributions of categorical data. No misleading elements are detected.

The y-axis starts at 0 but has a maximum of 1200, while the actual data range appears to be approximately 800-1050 deaths per 100,000. This compressed scale exaggerates the visual differences between bars, making relatively modest increases in death rates appear more dramatic than they actually are.

The chart uses two y-axes with different scales (left axis: 0-10% for bars, right axis: 0-100% for line), making direct visual comparison misleading. Additionally, departments are ordered by bar height rather than a logical sequence, which exaggerates the perceived differences and creates a false impression of a natural progression.

The y-axis begins at 104 instead of 0, visually exaggerating the relatively small fluctuations in the team's offensive rating (ranging from ~103.5 to 108). This truncation makes the modest 4-point variation appear as dramatic swings in performance.

The y-axis begins at 1,100 students rather than zero, visually exaggerating the growth in enrollment over the 20-year period. This truncation makes the increase from 1,200 to 1,900 students appear much more dramatic than it actually is proportionally.

The chart uses two side-by-side bar charts with different y-axis scales ($1.4 trillion labeled on left vs. unlabeled scale on right), making direct visual comparison misleading. Additionally, the right chart's y-axis appears to extend beyond $1.4 trillion despite the same label, exaggerating the visual representation of the rightmost bar and creating an inappropriate axis range that distorts the perception of relative values between the two panels.

The y-axis does not start at zero and appears to begin around .200 or higher, visually exaggerating the differences between batting averages that range only from .238 to .275. This truncation makes modest variations appear much more dramatic than they actually are.

The legend uses binning ranges with drastically different widths: the first bin (>10000) is unbounded, while subsequent bins range from 9000 units (1000-10000) down to just 8 units (1-9). This inconsistent binning obscures the true distribution of values across regions and can exaggerate differences in the lower ranges relative to the upper ranges.

This is an appropriate use of a pie chart showing part-to-whole composition of favorite colors among 80 friends. The labeled percentages sum to 100%, the slice sizes accurately represent their labeled values, and the chart type is suitable for displaying categorical preference data as proportions of a whole.

The pie chart slice sizes do not accurately represent their labeled percentages. The 18-59 age group is labeled at 56% but visually occupies approximately 70-75% of the pie, while the 60+ group at 35% appears significantly smaller than it should relative to the 56% slice. The visual areas are substantially misaligned with the stated values.

The choropleth map uses a 3D effect with county-level boundaries and shading that creates visual distortion, making it difficult to accurately compare values across regions. The perspective and layering of the map projection can exaggerate or minimize perceived differences in the evapotranspiration fractions between counties.

This choropleth map uses a sequential color gradient to represent Linux desktop market share percentages by country, with clearly defined ranges in the legend (6-12.3%, 3-6%, 2-3%, 1.5-2%, 1-1.5%, <1%, and 'Not enough data'). The color intensity appropriately corresponds to the data values, the categories are clearly labeled, and the visualization is suitable for geographic data distribution. No misleading elements are detected.

The y-axis extends from -10,000 to 20,000, with a large portion of negative values that are not relevant to the data being displayed. This unnecessarily compresses the actual data (which appears to range near zero to a few hundred) into a small visual space, making trends difficult to discern. The axis range should be set to appropriately show the actual data distribution without excessive negative space.

The legend shows inconsistent binning intervals for conflict distribution: '0 to 50' (50 unit range), '50 to 1,000' (950 unit range), '1,000 to 5,000' (4,000 unit range), '5,000 to 10,000' (5,000 unit range), and '10,000 to 100,000' (90,000 unit range). These vastly unequal bin widths make visual comparison misleading, as color intensity differences do not represent proportional differences in conflict counts.

This dot plot appropriately displays demographic correlates of cell phone use across three time periods (2007, 2009, 2013) with a continuous x-axis starting at 0 and showing percentages. The chart type is suitable for comparing multiple categories and time points, the axis is properly scaled, and the color coding clearly distinguishes between years without distortion.

The 'Binary Brexit Map' on the left uses only two colors (yellow and blue) to represent Brexit support, creating a false dichotomy that oversimplifies regional voting patterns. The legend on the right shows a continuous gradient from <10% to >60%, but the left map cannot accurately represent this nuanced data with just two colors, resulting in significant misrepresentation of the actual distribution of Brexit support across regions.

The legend shows binning intervals with drastically unequal widths, particularly the final category (2,811-298,937) which spans approximately 296,000 cases compared to earlier bins spanning only 100-200 cases. This inconsistent binning obscures the true distribution and makes visual comparison misleading, as the darkest color represents an enormous range that could encompass vastly different case counts.

A pie chart is used to represent percentages (58%, 70%, 60%, 63%) that do not sum to 100% and are not parts of a meaningful whole. These appear to be individual performance metrics or ratings for different people, which are not suitable for part-to-whole comparison in a pie chart format.

The color-coded legend uses inconsistent binning sizes (75,000+, 10,000-30,000, 1,000-9,999, 1-999) with unequal intervals that don't properly represent the data ranges. Additionally, the visual representation of state colors does not accurately correspond to the labeled case counts, as the color assignments appear arbitrary relative to the stated case ranges, making it difficult to accurately interpret case severity by state.

The bar/rectangle sizes do not accurately represent the labeled population values. For example, 2001 (10000) and 2002 (11000) show only a 10% difference in population, but their visual sizes are dramatically different, with 2002 appearing significantly larger. Similarly, 2004 (14500) and 2005 (15000) have only a 3.4% difference but are shown with vastly different visual areas, with 2005 appearing much larger than warranted by the data.

This line chart appropriately displays weekly COVID-19 admission rates over time for different age groups. The y-axis starts at 0, uses consistent tick intervals, and the line chart is suitable for showing trends across sequential time periods. The chart accurately represents the data without distortion or exaggeration.

The y-axis is inverted, with 0% at the top and 100% at the bottom, which reverses the perceived trend. This makes improving access to safe drinking water appear as a decline rather than an improvement, fundamentally distorting the data's message.

The y-axis starts at 0.0000 and extends only to 0.0030, which is an extremely compressed range relative to the data values shown. While the axis technically begins near zero, the narrow range combined with the small absolute values being displayed creates a visual distortion that exaggerates the differences between countries' gun death rates. The chart would be more honestly presented with a y-axis that better reflects the scale of the underlying data.

The y-axis starts at 0°F but the data range is between approximately 55-60°F, with the plotting area beginning around 50°F. This truncation of the axis exaggerates the visual magnitude of temperature variations that are actually quite small (less than 1°F over 135 years). A properly scaled axis would make the trend less dramatic and more accurately represent the data.

The bar lengths do not accurately represent the labeled percentages. Category A is labeled 26% but appears to extend beyond what would be proportionally correct relative to the 0%-100% axis scale. The visual sizes of the bars do not match their stated values, creating a misrepresentation of the data.

The visual comparison between the 'BEFORE' ($186/mo) and 'AFTER' ($64/mo) sections uses disproportionate space allocation that exaggerates the savings. Additionally, the vibrant pink coloring and checkmark graphics in the 'AFTER' section use design elements to further amplify the perceived benefit beyond what the numerical data alone would suggest.

The y-axis starts at 0 but the scale is compressed, ranging only from 0 to 1.5 meters. Since all bars cluster between approximately 1.4 and 1.5 meters, this narrow range visually exaggerates minor differences in minimum heights between states and genders. A more appropriate axis range would better represent the actual data distribution.

The y-axis starts at 0 but the scale appears compressed, with the maximum value around 12.0. However, the visual spacing and grid lines create an exaggerated representation of differences between bars. The truncated upper range and uneven visual scaling make small differences between complaint rates appear more dramatic than they actually are.

The chart uses 3D effects that distort visual comparison between the bars, making the 1950-2008 bar appear disproportionately larger than it should relative to the 2009-2016 bar. The pie chart shows three segments labeled 19%, 22%, and 38%, which sum to only 79% rather than 100%. The bar lengths do not accurately represent the labeled values. A pie chart is inappropriate for comparing market values across two time points (2018 vs 2028), as these are not parts of a single whole. The x-axis displays dates in a non-sequential order (28Apr2020, 27Apr2020, 29Apr2020, 01May2020, etc.), which disrupts the natural temporal progression and makes it difficult to identify trends over time. The chart uses two different y-axes with vastly different scales (left axis: 0-4,500,000 for person cooling degree days; right axis: 0-100% for AC ownership), making direct visual comparison between the two metrics misleading. The 3D perspective effect distorts the visual comparison of slice sizes, making front-facing slices appear larger than back-facing ones regardless of their actual percentages. The chart uses a 3D pie chart, which distorts visual comparison through perspective effects—the red 'Gifts' section appears disproportionately large due to the 3D angle. A pie chart is inappropriate for this data because the values (275, 261, 214 miles) represent absolute trip lengths, not parts of a meaningful whole. The pie chart segments do not visually match their labeled percentages. The chart uses a 3D pie/donut effect that distorts visual comparison of the values, making it difficult to accurately assess the relative magnitudes of the different metrics. The pie chart slices do not visually correspond to their labeled percentages. The y-axis uses a logarithmic scale (1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000) rather than a linear scale, which distorts visual comparison of bar heights and exaggerates differences between smaller values. This line chart displays categorical survey responses about custom maps, not sequential or time-series data. The chart uses two y-axes with different scales (left axis: 0-90 for % Affordable to Millennials; right axis: 32-48 for Absolute Mobility), making direct visual comparison between the two metrics misleading. The bar for Wayne Enterprises (22%) is visually much longer than the bar for Stark Industries (78%), despite representing a significantly smaller percentage. A line chart is used to display categorical year-over-year data rather than sequential continuous data. The y-axis is inverted, with 0 at the top and 4 at the bottom, which reverses the intuitive perception of the data. This is a stacked area chart showing percentage composition of film genres over time from 1908 to 2008. The y-axis correctly starts at 0% and goes to 100%, with evenly spaced intervals. The chart uses physical objects of varying shapes and sizes to represent prices, but the visual heights and volumes do not accurately correspond to the labeled monetary values. The stacked bar chart displays procedures per 1,000 population, but the individual segment lengths do not accurately represent their labeled values. This line chart appropriately displays time-series data (diarrhea-related deaths from July 2002 to December 2010) with a y-axis starting at zero, consistent tick intervals, and proper use of multiple lines to compare age groups. This line chart appropriately displays mean life expectancy trends over time for multiple countries. This line chart appropriately displays mortality risk trends over time with a y-axis starting at 0, consistent tick intervals, and suitable sequential data representation. The bar chart displays global television audiences for sports events with a y-axis starting at 0 and using consistent, evenly-spaced intervals (0, 100, 200, 300, etc.). The cheese bar (first bar) is visually disproportionately tall compared to its labeled value. This choropleth map uses appropriate color gradation to represent continuous percentage data by county, with a clear legend showing the scale from 0 to 40%+. The y-axis begins at $280 rather than $0, which exaggerates the visual differences between the two fare lines and makes fluctuations appear more dramatic than they actually are. This truncation artificially inflates the perceived volatility of airfare prices over the 40-year period.

Pie charts are designed to show parts of a whole that sum to 100%, but these charts appear to represent market share or distribution data across different companies/providers where the visual representation may not accurately convey the proportions. Additionally, comparing two pie charts side-by-side (left vs. right) makes it difficult to assess differences in values, as pie slices are inherently harder to compare across charts than bar lengths would be. The bar chart appropriately displays categorical data (programming languages/expressions) with a y-axis starting at zero and evenly spaced tick intervals. The line chart appropriately displays continuous data (movie runtime in minutes over time) with a properly scaled y-axis starting at 0, evenly spaced tick intervals, and sequential x-axis data (years from 1950-2014). This line chart appropriately displays time-series data (1970-2005) with a continuous sequential variable on the x-axis and a properly scaled y-axis starting at 0. This line chart appropriately displays survival rates over time with a y-axis starting at 0 and evenly spaced tick intervals. This bar chart appropriately displays favorable response percentages by country with the x-axis starting at 0, consistent bar widths, and proper color coding by percentage ranges. This stacked horizontal bar chart appropriately displays natural gas sourcing data with the x-axis starting at zero and using consistent, evenly-spaced tick intervals.
